video_title: Most Investors Will Never Beat The Market
transcript: [music] >> Welcome to a special series of the Meb Faber Show on the past, [music] present, and future of America. I'm sitting down with some of the most notable historians, thinkers, and investors in US financial [music] history, all tied to my new coffee table book, Investing in America, The Rise of a 250-Year Bull Market, out July 4th. >> Meb Faber is the co-founder and chief investment officer at Cambria Investment Management. Due to industry regulations, he will not discuss any [music] of Cambria's funds on this podcast. All opinions expressed by podcast participants are solely their own opinions and do not reflect the opinion of Cambria Investment Management [music] or its affiliates. For more information, visit cambriainvestments.com. >> This episode is brought to you by Ivy Invest. You know I've talked a lot on the show about endowment-style investing, the Yale model, the Swensen playbook, public markets, private equity, private credit, real estate, all blended together into a portfolio built for the long game. The data on this approach is pretty compelling. The problem has always been the same. It was basically impossible for regular investors to actually do it. Well, here's somebody who lived through that world from the inside. Wendy Lee spent 18 years managing multi-billion-dollar endowment funds in New York, and at some point she had a pretty simple realization. This is exactly how she had wanted to invest her own money, but even she couldn't access it as an individual. So, she built the thing that didn't exist. Ivy Invest is one of the first endowment-style funds designed for everyday investors. You get a single fund with exposure to private equity, real estate, private credit, and public markets. The whole mix starting at just a thousand bucks. I actually had Wendy on the podcast. Go back and listen to that episode. It's worth your time. For now, check them out at ivyinvest.co. That's ivyinvest.co. >> Before investing, carefully consider the investment objective, risk, charges, and expenses of the institutional investment strategy fund. The prospectus contains this and other information. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Learn more at ivyinvest.co. >> Welcome back, everybody. Today's guest is a true legend, Charlie Ellis. Charlie founded Greenwich Associates in 1972, written over 20 books, long-time member of Yale's investment committee. He's out with a new book, Great American Investments, a history of the bold initiatives that shaped a nation. 14 audacious public investments that built modern America from the Louisiana Purchase to the Marshall Plan. Charlie, welcome to the show. >> Nice to be here. Thanks. >> You know, we're going to get to your new book in a minute, but I feel amiss. You've written so many books. I've read most of them. And so we got to go back to the wayback machine and talk about one of them. And I'm guessing you think we're going to talk about Loser's Game, but we're not. We're going to talk about a different book I have, listeners, which was published man, this has got to be 50 years ago, The Repurchase of Common Stock. Do you remember writing this? >> I do. I You know, I'd just come out of Harvard Business School, and I was three, four years out, and I had this brain thrust. You know, American companies during the Second World War could not raise capital with equity offerings easily, so they borrowed money, and they borrowed money, and they borrowed money, because a lot of their business was government contracts, that was a reasonably safe and sensible thing to be doing. But they had an enormous amount of debt. And they said, "We've got to pay that debt down." So from roughly 1946 to 1965, they paid debt down, paid debt down, paid debt down. You get policies going and it's very hard to stop them. And they'd pay the debt down so much that way under-leveraged, inappropriate for the equity investors, was the balance sheet. And my pitch was, "You really ought to borrow money and repurchase shares to get her back to the right balance, to get long-term returns for investors." >> [laughter] >> Nobody had done anything like that for so long. It was considered groundbreaking at the time and a real innovation. And fortunately, nobody knew that I'd just graduated from school, so they wouldn't have otherwise they would dismissed it, but it caught on. And one of the reasons it caught on is that a fellow at Syracuse University wrote to me and said, "I've done some work in this area. You've done some work. Why don't we put our work together and create a book?" So, we did. And that was kind of exciting. And then the guys at Goldman Sachs at the trading desk said, "Hey, look at this. There's a book out that says we are on the right track if we go to companies and say, 'We will help you at 40 cents a share commission. We will help you buy in stock cuz you're over equity.'" And they sent copies of the book as a legitimizer out to a thousand corporations. Then pretty soon they were doing a brisk business. Unfortunately for me and my friend, the book itself did not sell particularly well and we never got any royalties cuz we didn't get up to the minimum sales, but we had a wonderful experience and it got me off to writing books and writing articles and feeling good about things. And since then, well, it's been a boom. Share repurchasing goes over a trillion dollars today. And it's accepted as being a normal part of sensible management, which I find thrilling and I'm glad I lived long enough to see it all come together. >> Well, it's incredible cuz we'll often see politicians or gurus or someone talk about all this stock repurchases used to be illegal and no one did these before like 20 years ago. And it's funny that cuz I'll I'll just I'll look at them and I'll I'll say to myself, "That's funny cuz I have a book." So, maybe there was a time machine or something, but I'm pretty sure someone wrote this about 50 years ago. And you also wrote an article about it. There's a lot of people that quote get it. You know, Buffett probably talks about it more than anybody. There's his annual letters from the '80s talking about share repurchases. But like you mentioned, it's become in most years now, I think if not all years, buybacks on average will outpace dividends. And it's an interesting as it's become just a huge part of the culture. Now it's spreading around the world. You're starting to see it in the UK and Japan and Korea and China. You ever thought when you wrote this that it would be such a big piece of corporate finance kind of 101? >> Not even close. But it is fun to see something that you did think was a pretty sensible idea start to take hold and then other people have really carried the ball and run with it. >> How do you think about the general knowledge and take on, you know, corporate how they deal with cash? Is it a battle we can win? Is it frustrating? Is it something that, you know, what are your thoughts? >> A company management is really quite sharp, thoughtful, and focused on the long term most of the time. Every once in a while, and then of course these events get picked up by newspapers cuz they're interesting and celebrity. Every once in a while somebody does something that's really not exactly right. There are times when it may not look quite right, but that's more often than not just coincidental. Most of the time it's a very sensible, disciplined, rather formulaic process driven by the board of directors more than by the internal management and very much on interest of serving the shareholders. You know, as a as an investor myself, I would rather people did more share buybacks than less dividends. But most people, honestly, as you were saying earlier, really love getting dividends. They see that as free money, spending opportunity. Who said yes part of their family life? As individuals, I can certainly understand that. One of the interesting phenomena is that some institutions still wait endowment of payouts in dividends more valuable than accretions in stock value. I guess that's a matter of personal opinion, but in the long run, accretion of the value of the stocks is much more important, particularly after tax. >> As we hop forward to what you're probably most famous book, as we walk forward the last 50-plus years, the Vanguard [snorts] indexing, this massive trend towards low fees, how you feeling here in 2026? You know, is this the product proliferation on a dollar-weighted basis seems to be that most things are going to low cost, but still you see people paying 1, 2% you see a lot of the particular with the privates charging quite a bit more, and here we are with this new cycle of a lot of public companies, these big dudes coming in the trillions. With SPVs and all these structures, how you feeling about the investing world today? Any just broad thoughts? >> Well, first of all, it's a wonderful phenomenon that every American family and people from other countries as well can invest in the companies that make America's economy go and grow and grow. That doesn't mean they can't make mistakes. Individuals make lots of mistakes over and over and over again. There's a field of economics that I find fascinating, behavioral economics, where it serious-minded economists in years gone by used to believe that all individuals act rationally in their own best interest. And people in behavioral economics say, "Well, that may be true in general most of the time for most people, but let me tell you I can show you over and over and over again that people misunderstand reality and behave on that basis of that misunderstanding. For an example, and then they'll go through a string of things like how many college people would say they're above average dancers? How many would say they're above average investors? How many are above average at making friends? How much How many are above average at listening well for understanding? How many people that over and over and over again. And invariably more than 50% should say they are above average at the best, but usually it runs somewhere around 80, 85, even 90% in some cases, such as young males at trading stocks. And that causes people to believe in things that are just not true, but believe in themselves, which is a positive, I suppose. Better than everybody being optimistic than everybody being pessimistic, but it takes them off in directions of making mistakes. And that's part of the American system, and it is true of every other market around the world. More likely true in underdeveloped markets than it is in fairly sophisticated markets like the US, the UK, Canada, even Germany. It varies from one country to another, some, but most countries and most people, it's a phenomenon that we think better of ourselves than we actually are. And I'm one of those, too. A wonderful man named Daniel Kahneman, who won the Nobel Prize, wrote a superb book, Thinking Fast and Slow. And everybody who wants to be an investor and succeed as an investor should take time to read that book. I think it's the best book for investors ever written. >> I like it. All right. Well, that's as you can see on my on my shelf behind me, I've read just about every book on there except for that one. For the life of me, for some reason it hasn't entered the stack yet. So, I'll I'll I'll add that to the list. >> You will love it. >> What should we be doing for these young folks to try to get them on the the straight and narrow, the right path of investing for the long run, and not doing everything in your own power to destroy your chances of success? >> Boy, is that an important question, and I think there is an overwhelmingly clear-cut answer. Doesn't mean that anybody's going to follow it, but it is the really right thing to do. Let's assume you're 25, you come out of college, maybe did a little graduate work, and you're starting to earn some money and save. Saving is terribly important to investing. You can't do investing unless you do saving. Fine. Okay, what is long-term? Well, you're 25, you'll probably live to 85, maybe 90 by the time we see what medical science can do to advance our longevity. Let's say 85 just for convenience. That gives you 60 years. 60 years is your long-term. Now, if you're lucky and you have a happy marriage and raise children that live on beyond you, you might want to think more than 60 years. You might want to think 80, 90, 100, 125, something like that. But, let's stay with just your own life. If you save money and invest it and let it compound, and compounding is, Charlie Munger and Warren Buffett and David Swensen and every other great investor would tell you, is the great magic of investing. Compounding grows steadily. 1 2 4 8 16 32 64 128. Hey, I want that ladder part. That's 64 to 128 in one round. Jeepers, I'd love that. How do I get there? Start early. That's all you have to do and stay in there. And if you will think long-term, you will find yourself coming to certain conclusions. One is that this is really a good deal. Two, you don't know enough to know which individual stocks to own, so you ought to do a diversified portfolio. Better than that, you can do a diversified portfolio through an index fund, and so you pay very, very small fee that takes away would take away from your rate of compounding and leave it in the investment and let it compound away for you. That's terrific. Okay. What else can you do? Avoid taxes. Avoid taxes by just hanging on. Don't buy and sell, just hang on. Well, what do you want to hang on to? A diversified index fund will do you wonders. If you just leave it alone and let it grow, let it grow, and let it grow larger and larger and larger, faster and faster and faster. And over that time period, that long-term, you will be really well off. But if you fiddle around or try to help yourself by making some smart moves or try to do something clever, the odds are high that you will make a mistake. And if you make a mistake, it'll cut in. In any event, you're going to wind up paying taxes that you didn't need to be paying. And you probably will make mistakes that will cost you. The average investor in an average year loses 2% of their return. Look at this just for a second. If you say, "7% seems like maybe a little optimistic, but seems like a reasonable prospect for the longer term." If you start there, you say, "Well, we've got 3% inflation." I know it's more now, but on average 3% looks to be sort of the higher end of inflation expectation. So, that 7% has already been cut to 4%. Jeepers, if I lose another 2% by making mistakes, I'm down from 4% to 2%. Then jeepers, if I pay a active manager to run my portfolio, that's going to be another percent. I'm coming up with damn near nothing. This is not good deal. Okay. You can get right back to the good deal by saying, "I'm going to index and let it float." And to the extent I can shelter by doing it in my 401k, I will do exactly that. And I will allow time to work for me. Money making money for me is all very easy cuz it works wonderfully and you don't have to do anything except not do anything. And avoiding temptation to help and improve and make things work out better is the first and biggest secret about long-term investing success. The second thing is to avoid taxes and fees to the extent you can on a realistic basis. And the third is to leave it alone. Leave it alone. >> Your new book, Charlie, I loved it. Of course I love this book cuz it's on topic that is near and dear to my heart as we close in our 250th anniversary of this great country. So, what was the inspiration? You know, every time I write a book I just have never doing that again and you've done way more than I have. So, what was the inspiration to put pen to paper again? >> My wife turned to me and said one day, "Do you know COVID is really serious? Nobody knows exactly how serious, but we know it's really serious. So, I've made a decision for our family and I hope that you will agree to it. Good marriage? Of course. Yes, ma'am. I think we should stay inside the house in isolation, not have any friends over, not go out, no movies, no dining out. We stay in the house unless we have to go out for some working purpose. Now, you're going to be stir crazy in no time because you're a guy with a lot of energy and always trying to do things. So, you're going to have to find something to keep you busy while we all stay inside the house. And I went to sleep thinking, I don't know how to solve that problem. I woke up and I had a brainstorm, just a lucky, lucky brainstorm. Over the years, the American people have made investments that have really made our country a wonderful place to live. It's a wonderful for us that we have Social Security. It's a wonderful thing for us that we bought the Louisiana Purchase and Alaska. It's a wonderful thing for us that we have more national parks than the rest of the world added together. It's a wonderful thing for us that we have more colleges and universities than the whole rest of the world. It's a wonderful thing for us that we have the National Institutes of Health, which is the largest funder of of research that leads towards health improvements and longevity of life. Major positive phenomenon. And it has really worked out well for America. It's great that I we have Social Security so that we don't have what we look like we were going to have is old age homes where people get stashed like cordwood because they don't have any money and the community can't afford to pay for it. The real problems. Really terrific thing that after the Second World War we had the national commitment to the GI Bill where a whole bunch of young people got terrific educations in college or if they were not aiming for college but wanted a better way of life, industrial jobs and learned all kinds of skills training. Terrific a surge in the whole economic phenomenon. It's terrific that we have more art museums than the rest of the world. These investments have made America what it's all about. In many ways and I think all of us should take great satisfaction and pride that we have so many outstanding positive factors. Then I thought, you know, each one of these might have an interesting background and my story, how the hell did they happen? Got a researcher to work with me and we dug into the internet and the files at Yale University and pulled up all kinds of information on each one of these. And then I read through them all and sorted out the important facts and the storyline and sure enough each one is basically the same story. One or two, maybe three people got excited about an idea, a dream that they sure believed could come true. And worked their way through the democratic decision making process, got government approval, made it happen and now we've gotten the benefits. And those benefits have changed America in ways that all of us would look and say, you know, if you ever took away those dozen different phenomena, it would really hurt the value of our life experience as citizens in this wonderful country. Isn't it great that these things were done? It's really fun to read because first of all it's very optimistic book. It profiles a lot of these ideas that are big, audacious, I mean crazy ideas at the time that as you mentioned championed by a few people and one of the themes that I kept coming back to was the obsessed individual. In the very first chapter, you talk about Social Security and there was a woman Francis or Fanny which I was laughing about cuz we had a a Fanny Fanny Farrington in our in our family, you know, a few generations above. You don't hear that name as much anymore but such a wonderful story of determination and drive that in many cases almost like if you would took a few of these people out you'd be like, "Wow, I wonder if that would have happened without some of these people." It feels impossible today but back then, you know, both sides of the aisle working together or even saying, "Hey, look, this is a cool project. Let's go do it." >> You know, it was not it wasn't easy even then. You know, we look back on history and we think that oh, you know, those decisions must have been easy to make. First of all, you had a terrific depression. And secondly, you had a very capable president in Franklin Roosevelt. And he was determined to come up with some really creative new ideas. Third, you had he as governor of New York had worked with Francis Perkins on some things in New York state and New York state in those days was a very open, assertive new ideas oriented kind of a state and then very big one and so that was visible and the real need was becoming obvious. More and more and more people were going to nursing homes then you see the numbers they would just jump jump jump jump faster and faster. The future would have been horrific. And then Roosevelt was smart enough to realize this is going to have to be a self-liquidating program. It's going to have to pay for itself because you will never be able to count on general revenue because if we do, it'll be subject to political reversions at any time over the next 15, 20, 50, 100 years. I want this to last forever. And then the Frances Perkins just happened to be one savvy cookie in a lot of different ways. She was a fascinating woman. She wore dark blue or black dresses with a white collar most of the time. Why? Because she thought in a man's world, men would see somehow [snorts] subliminally their mother and grandmother as advisers and they knew that their mothers were right and maybe that would be helpful. She kept in her pocketbook so she was never going to be an inconvenience to other people her last will and testament. So if she died on the traveling around the country, it'd all be there and organized in a lot sensible way. And she understood Franklin Roosevelt to be a kind of guy that made decisions and then after meeting with somebody else might change that decision and then meeting with somebody else might change that decision. She wanted to be sure he stayed right focused on getting this particular project done. So she met with him every week or so and would present him with a written memorandum one or two pages at length at the most and go through that item by item. Are you sure about this? Are you still comfortable with this? Does this work for you? And she'd get his reaffirming over and over and over again and one of the smart moves she made was she had a small number of very powerful people in the government to be on her committee to figure out what this program might be. And then made them work very conscientiously together so they could get the legislation organized and in time. And wonderfully capable and effective and trustworthy human being, she put every ounce of energy she had into making this happen, and it did. And the Social Security program has done more good for more people in America than any other government program in the history of the country. And if it hadn't been for this little old lady, and her determination and her skills, it would never have happened. Also wouldn't have happened if it hadn't been for Franklin Roosevelt supporting, and it wouldn't have happened at all, wouldn't even been considered if they hadn't been the depression. So, you get those three things together, yes, that's good luck, but then determination that make things work and to be effective comes right into play and isn't it marvelous that it works so very, very well. >> Do you think at some point the politicians will be able to modernize this a little bit, or they just going to not deal with it until it becomes a a big problem? >> Well, you can't deal with anything in politics until it's becomes a big problem or big opportunity. So, that's just being realistic. But, it has been changed. There have been a a gradual extension of when you could claim Social Security. There's a sad reality just for your listeners benefit that everybody ought to understand and pay attention to. You can claim Social Security at 62. You have to claim Social Security at 70 and a half. You and I might argue that those numbers ought to be pushed out, make them later and later. But, let me just focus on the difference between when you can claim and when you must claim. Smart people, thoughtful people, well-informed people would all wait until they were 70 and a half because the magnitude of increase in the benefits that you will have from there on, for the rest of your life, protected by inflation adjustments, so that's all their real money increases by 2/3. You get paid a lot more. And also, if you work longer, I'm still 88. I'm still working full tilt. I'm having a wonderful time. But I love my work. A lot of people don't love their work. But if they would work longer and keep putting money into their 401k instead of taking it out, the addition of the 401k and its natural growth and the increase, 76% increase in Social Security, combined would make a lot of people happy during their retirement in a way they will not be if they take Social Security at 62 and stop working at 65. >> One of the cool parts about this book, you know, you talk about these big projects and they span many different decades, centuries, but they had to get funded. Someone had to pay for them and they had very different structures. How important do you think, you know, a lot of these the structure, some of them were pretty unique. Some of them were riskier. How important were some of the structures, whether it's taxes, whether it's endowments, whether it's gifts, land grants, all these things, to the eventual success of the project? >> First, let's take a look at some of the differences. National Park, for example. Why were we able Those are beautiful, beautiful mountains, valleys. Isn't it exciting? Yes, it is. Why was that available? Well, candidly, Yellowstone is, for example, too high in altitude for raising stock, sheep, goats, cattle. Can't do it. And many of the mountains are just not decent for people to walk around in or build homes on. They're too steep, too rocky. So, one of the benefits was that the land for the national parks, in most cases, was dirt cheap. And that really does help. If you look at the Louisiana Purchase, we got unbelievably favorable terms. Napoleon was going to at one point, was going to take over the sugar islands, as they're called, in the Caribbean, money makers that the British had, and then he was going to attack through New Orleans and up the Mississippi River, moving towards Quebec and French-speaking Canada, and maybe be able to take the middle of this continent and make that part of his empire. And then he decided, you know, I'm spreading my resources a little bit too much. He also lost 20,000 soldiers to the yellow fever in the sugar islands, and his brother-in-law also died. Uh so, he decided, I'm going to renounce the whole idea of going after North America. I'm going to concentrate on Europe and try to beat the British and see if I can conquer most of Europe and make it into my empire. So, okay. So, a little bit of good luck that he decided not to stay in America and therefore was interested in getting rid of the French colony in New Orleans and nearby. So, he was ready to sell if he could get anything like a decent price. And so, the terms were made very, very friendly. And basically, it was like a long-term margin loan. We didn't put up any money. We got credit, and the credit didn't have to get cashed for a dozen years, and then gradually [clears throat] got cashed. Well, you know, to be able to make a real estate purchase and not put up any money for the first dozen years could make any kind of real estate just that much more attractive than in this case spectacularly more attractive. Helps to have a highly motivated seller and then it helps to have in Thomas Jefferson a motivated buyer and the combination worked out beautifully, but it was a marvelous bargain. And the Alaska was even worse as a part of more amazing as a bargain because the Crimean War had caused the Tsar to believe, you know, we're too extended. We've got too many things we're trying to control what we call Russian America, what we now call Alaska, is remote, unproductive, a whole bunch of small little pocket tribes living in valleys, very hard to collect taxes, and very expensive, and candidly it has a real risk that might cause us to get into war with the British because they control Canada or maybe the Americans cuz they're right behind them or maybe both of those guys. Let's find a way to sell it at any price. And so he had his ambassador working in Washington keen to sell when the American government said, "Uh, not so sure we want it. It's a long way away. Might be trouble. A lot of difficult to govern." But Governor Seward, who was Abraham Lincoln's Secretary of State, finally said, "You know, let's find a way to make this work out, but don't spend much money." So, it wasn't very expensive and it wasn't worth very much at the time. It was ridiculed as Seward's icebox and other kinds of throw away comments, but over the years it has been wonderful. When oil was discovered, boy, was a sensation. Gold was discovered, that was a sensation. Fish were found to be very, very plentiful and that was a sensation. There were all kinds of really wonderful advantages that have come our way as a result of buying Alaska for a song. >> One of the threads that I think is not appreciated enough, but you see through your book is everyone the narrative of the US being founded by a bunch of immigrants that were escaping religious persecution and searching for freedom, and of course that's true, you know, there's elements to that. But also, you know, in many ways America was funded, you know, there's a lot of investors. So if you look at even the early days of exploration with the Virginia Company, some of the Dutch companies, Sweden Company, Hudson Bay, those were not charities, right? Those were for-profit companies. And then you mentioned things like the early investments in purchasing Louisiana, Alaska, and then of course walking through to the modern day. There's something in the water, it feels like, with Americans. If you look at most of the worlds, they do surveys on countries in Europe and elsewhere about being entrepreneur, but also just at the percentage of investors that are equity owners. The US leads the charts on all of those, and granted part of that is just because we've had a great run, but also it just feels like there's something in the American spirit that is the risk-taking gene. That you talk to someone in Europe, say, "How do you invest?" They say, "Well, I earn interest in real estate." Or you talk to someone in Japan, and they're like, "What are you talking about? I Stocks haven't gone anywhere for 30 years. Why would I invest in stocks?" But you talk to the average American, and it very much feels like a different mentality. So it's fun just read through the book and see that, but also see how a lot of these projects were financed as well. >> One of the great things about American is people, and particularly wealthy people, reflect this very nicely also, is a very forward-looking point of view. People do have dreams, and they work hard to make those dreams come true. I think we could easily get into a nice agreement that there's a serious problem about the affluent or too affluent and straightforward middle America is not earning as large a fraction of the pie would be right for the long long run and has been true in the past. We've got a real problem. But it overall most Americans know that this is a land filled with opportunities. As you know, I'm very closely associated with Yale University. Partly because I was a student there, partly because I was a trustee, partly because my wife was the vice president and a lot of different connections. But if you look at Yale, it happens to be the most desirable undergraduate educational experience in the world. How many of the kids who come to Yale are on full scholarship? 100%. What do you mean? Full scholarship? No, no, no. They're on scholarship. Okay, how about full scholarship? It runs around 40%. 40% of the students at Yale are on a full scholarship? Yep. Need-blind admissions. If you have a family income of $120,000 or less, and that's well above the median American income, you will come to Yale without any charge. That kind of an attitude picked up and replicated at other great universities, Harvard, Princeton, Stanford, MIT, is really an exciting part of the American fabric. Universities are the engine for individuals to become great successes. Universities are also largely the most important engines of economic progress because of the research that's done in science, research that's done in all kinds of fields, engineering, particularly in science, but you look at that, typical of America, but not typical in other countries. That's part of the forward-looking point of view. And it's exciting to watch it in play. >> We just recorded a fun podcast. I'm sure you know and probably are buddies with Professor Getsman from Yale. And I was joking with him because much like you, I love collecting old books from authors that are hard to find. And he had one that was on the panic of 1720. And I couldn't find it anywhere, and I had to buy a copy from Canada for 250 bucks. And I was giving him a hard time. I was like, "Professor, you got to get this back in production cuz his books are they're great cuz they have a lot of art and and are beautiful just to flip through and look at these paintings and pictures from hundreds of years ago where people were talking about speculation, you know, like then and then these past experiences. And we're like, "Well, we haven't [clears throat] learned anything, you know, in some ways that these humans keep doing the same dumb stuff over and over again." >> That's part of the charm of human beings is that we are capable of continuing to plod along, making mistakes, and doing things creatively, and having fun, and breaking our hearts, and all the complexities that go with all the different feelings. >> We look at the market today, all-time highs, but you look at some of these surveys, which are kind of odd, like the Michigan, and people are reporting they're they're miserable. And it's always curious to see what the actual reasons are cuz inflation, at least relative to history, like you mentioned, not that high, low unemployment, the economy is booming. But you of course you have this new influence, which is your phone and devices and social media and everyone becoming more polarized in their own tribes. Is the US willing to underwrite some of these long-duration projects anymore? Are you hopeful? Are you worried? Cuz if I read the book, a lot of these examples are from kind of the early years. There's not a whole lot of modern ones. Internet, you could argue maybe is is one, but even that had its origins in the I think the '60s. How you feeling about some of these projects? Any good ones to point to that you think maybe might be a little more modern give us hope? >> Sure, artificial intelligence would be the natural one to book on. Could make a tremendous positive transforming difference for the economy and for the average experience of the typical American. Could be spectacular. It also could be unbelievably important for the top one half of 1/10 of 1% who happen to be right on top of the whole wave. And it could also be, candidly, some real difficulty for people at work as their jobs get vaporized. Don't need them any longer cuz AI will take care of it. We don't know yet how fast it's going to happen, but my fear, personally, is it's more likely to happen faster than expected rather than slower than projected. >> It feels like there's a real sense of civic duty, of service, like of people who looked at it and said I want to contribute. I want to make this country better. I have these ideas that I'm promoting. In the modern world, how do we get young people to carry on that torch, to get enthusiastic about that concept? It feels maybe very tribal at this point, but is there any ways you think as you read back through these examples? And to me, it's super inspiring. Be like, "Wow, oh my god, the Erie Canal." I look at that and read how it was described in the day and be like, "This literally is impossible. Why would they even do this?" You know, and then it kind of happened. But even with the example of Francis and with Fanny, even examples like Mellon and FDR, the National Gallery, like arguably didn't really like each other, and yet you had this amazing outcome. Is there anything we think, you know, as we think about this whole book and the optimistic thread line for young people today? >> Oh, sure. You have to go back to Alexis de Tocqueville in the mid-18th 19th century, a long time ago, almost 200 years ago, he was writing about Americans volunteer to all kinds of things, and it's true today. If the Peace Corps is one illustration, it's been around for a long time now, but it's still vibrant and active. But go to any community and you find there are people volunteering to do Boy Scouts and Girl Scouts. There are people volunteering at their church. There are people volunteering at the food bank. There are people volunteering for all kinds of different organizations that are trying to serve useful purposes and make things better in their community, make things better for more people. And it's very deep part of America, and it candidly, it's quite rewarding for the individuals on a spiritual basis, and therefore keen to do it over and over and over again. I laugh looking at my family, my children, my wife, myself, my parents, uncles and aunts, cousins, they all were serious about volunteering in one or another way or another to make things better in one activity or another activity. Sometimes for education, sometimes for health care, sometimes for the way the community is organized, but volunteering is a deeply rooted phenomenon in America. And the other thing that's very closely associated with that is philanthropic giving. Very large fraction of Americans dig in to their pockets to do things that they consider important philanthropically. Big chunk of that, of course, is through churches and synagogues, but another big chunk of it goes right into the community activities. Community Chest being the most obvious illustration. And it's part of the American way of doing things, and it's going to continue, I hope, forever and ever and ever cuz it's a beautiful beautiful part of what makes this country wonderful. >> It would be interesting to see as, you know, the kind of AI develops if there is a significant impact on particularly younger entry-level employment. It would be great to see a a renewed focus on some of those programs that you mentioned as an option for young people say, "Hey, look, you know, I could go do Peace Corps or something similar for a couple years, get some additional skills while learning and continuing to look for a job, but as a program that you know, not mandatory, but as an option for people that may be struggling to make the jump to this new weird reality that we're going to have here in in the next few years. Charlie, we're going to wind down with a few more questions. This has been such a blessing to have you today. If you think back in a couple years after this book comes out and you're like, "You know what? I definitely got to add a chapter 15." You mentioned AI. Are there any other ones you think might be candidates? Space exploration? What else might be a future US >> [laughter] >> NASA is one of the chapters. And of course, that was driven by Jack Kennedy's recognition that the world would be astonished by what human beings did. Eisenhower, boy, Eisenhower is a brilliant man, very thoughtful. One of his favorite ways to have a nice time was to invite a group of scientists to come and talk about what they were doing and why it was interesting. Uh so, he's increasingly appreciated as a very effective president, but he did not think that it made sense economically to use human beings in space. He thought instrumentation would be fine. But Kennedy understood that psychologically or spiritually or publicity would be much much greater if you had people doing things. And so that's why he backed NASA so fully. Uh NASA's still doing exciting things, although it's less and less as a percentage of GNP, it's still a big phenomenon and America leads the space race in space activity. And people really love seeing it. If you watch the internet is another one of those government programs. It cost less than a million dollars to do all of the work that led to the internet. The rest of it's been voluntary work by individuals saying I've got ways of making internet better. Does anybody not use the internet today? I don't think so. And the number of people who use the internet 50 times every year, every day rather, 50 times every day. That's an exciting reality and it's all because of a government investment in order to develop a set of skills that might be useful from a point of view of defense then they might have might have civilian application. Of course the civilian application has gone zooming right past any military activity that it's one of the most important changes that's taken place in our society in the last 10 or 20 years. The fact that it was a very low cost operation doesn't take away from the fact that we all use it now. >> [laughter] >> All right, Charlie, if you look back on your career, you got to think about this one for a minute. It could be a good investment, it could be a bad investment, but what has been your most memorable investment over the years? And you can pick a couple if you're having a hard time. >> Well, do two. One is indexing. I believe deeply in indexing. And my wife, my sons, grandchildren, and I all do indexing. Far and away the best investment I ever had was an amazing piece of very good luck. I happened to have learned through the jungle drums that there was a terrific guy doing really creative long-term investing that was just wonderful, wonderfully capable. And I went to lunch with a man that I had admired because he was a great investor, but he also was chief executive of a firm that our firm Greenwich Associates had been consulting with, and we had very clear documentation that they should change their business strategy. And he wanted me to come in for lunch, and I was thrilled because we documented the change needed to be made, but the firm did not make the change, and then did not make the change. And 2 years had gone by, and I was feeling frustrated because we had a winning proposition. So when he said he'd like to have lunch, I was thrilled cuz I know I was going to be able to get the chief executive saying, "Now listen, you guys, I want you to use all this great stuff coming from Greenwich Associates." So got off the elevator, went to his office, and his assistant said, "Oh, Mr. Gottesman would be keen to see you right away." I went in to see him, and Sandy Gottesman is a wonderful, kind, thoughtful, very well-informed man. He said, "Charlie, I just want to be sure. The reason I wanted to have lunch with you is I wanted to tell you man to man, not over the telephone, then not with a letter, that we're never going to use your service. And that's because your service is based on the judgments and opinions of the thousand largest companies, and we don't serve those companies. We serve smaller companies, entrepreneurial companies, and the entrepreneurs that own those companies. So your market and our market are like ships in the dark passing each other at night. We don't see each other, we don't know each other. There's no real overlap. So, just want you to know we're not going to use the service. So, all of a sudden I'm stuck with I've come to have lunch with Sandy Gottesman, smart guy, very capable, and he just completed the conversation by telling me it's all over. We got in the elevator and said, I've got to think of something. I've got to think of something. I've got to think of something to ask him about when we sit down for lunch. As we sat down for lunch, he handed to me the the menu and I had a menu. We're looking at them and all of a sudden I thought, of course, the obvious question. Sandy, I said, you're one of the best investors in America. What's your favorite long-term investment? He'll look at me and say, Berkshire. I'm sorry, Sandy, I didn't hear what you said. Berkshire. Is Would you mind repeating one more time? Yes, Berkshire Hathaway. Warren Buffett. Click. Sandy, do me a big favor. Tell me how you look at us as long-term investment. He started talking about it and after about 10 minutes had laid out a fascinating case for Berkshire Hathaway. This was in the early 1970s and I said, Gee, it sounds wonderful. How long do you plan to own it? Forever. I'm sorry, Sandy, what did you say? I said, forever. And he did. And his wife, when he had died, she left in her will a billion dollars to New York University to make its medical school tuition-free. I went back to my partners and said, we have set aside some money in case we have a bad year. We don't want to call everybody and say you got to put more money up. This is a little bitty firm. We could have a bad year very easily. Early 1970s was a tough period. I think we ought to have a side fund that was invested sensibly for all of us. If it works out fine, but if the market somehow goes down more and our clients cut away, we got to have money to keep this company going. And I recommended we put together what turned out to be a small kitty as a side fund. We decided or I decided cuz the other guys didn't understand investing particularly well. I said, "Charlie, you know something about investing, so why don't you decide how we'll invest it?" I said, "We're going to put it all in Berkshire Hathaway." All in one company? I said, "Well, it's a diversified company itself and a really smart guy managing it and he thinks very creatively about long-term investing and it's had a very nice record. Jesus, sounds risky to me. Well, how much does it cost? Pretty expensive per share. It's about $700. One of the guys said, "Oh my god, $700. That sounds like a really high price." And then "No, no, no, you don't understand. They just didn't divide it. If they divided it by 10, it would be $70 and you'd think it was a reasonable price." "Oh, I guess you're right." So, we put the money into Berkshire Hathaway. And then it went up and up and up and up. Then it's gone up not quite but almost a hundred times in the 50 years. Now, 50 years is a long time, but it's nothing like a hundred times multiple on a value. And so, everything that I can imagine positive has happened at Berkshire Hathaway because of Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger and the discipline with which they followed investing and thinking about long-term and compounding.
